summary: Dan Luu examines why his personal blog often gets more traffic than corporate engineering blogs from well-funded tech companies, despite those companies having more resources and greater incentive to succeed. Through interviews with companies that have compelling blogs (Cloudflare, Heap, Segment) and those that don't, he finds that successful corporate blogs share a lightweight approval process with direct executive support, while unsuccessful ones get bogged down in months-long approval cycles with multiple stakeholders who perceive only risk and no benefit from streamlining. The key difference is high-level executives willing to take responsibility and cut through bureaucratic risk aversion, enabling engineers to publish interesting technical content quickly.
key_points: Successful corporate engineering blogs have fast approval processes (hours to weeks) with few approvers, mainly from engineering
Unsuccessful blogs have slow, multi-stakeholder approval processes (3-6 months typical, up to a year) that make posts vaguer and less interesting
High-level executive support (CTO, CEO, co-founder level) is critical to maintaining a lightweight process and overriding bureaucratic risk aversion
The natural state of engineers writing about their work is interesting; corporations have to actively suppress this to create boring blogs
Individual stakeholders in large companies perceive risk from streamlining but no personal benefit, creating a structural barrier to improvement
money_quotes: In order to have a boring blog, the corporation has to actively stop engineers from putting interesting content out there.
My opinion is that the natural state of a corp eng blog where people get a bit of feedback is a pretty interesting blog.

None of the fourteen stakeholders who'd have to sign off on approving a streamlined process care about streamlining the process since that would be good for the company in a way that doesn't really impact them, not when that would mean seemingly taking responsibility for the risk a streamlined process would add, however small.
ICs in large companies don't have the authority or don't feel like they have the authority to do something just because it makes sense.
One comment I've often heard from people at more bureaucratic companies is something like 'every company our size is like this', but that's not true.
key_insight: Corporate engineering blogs fail not from lack of talent or material, but because bureaucratic risk aversion without executive intervention transforms potentially interesting technical content into months-delayed, watered-down fluff that engineers hate writing and nobody wants to read.
